text.title: Pitch Formats, Audience Targeting, and Objection Handling
text.summary: Elevator pitch template, audience-specific pitch structures, LAER objection handling model, and the most common pitching antipatterns.

section.title: Elevator Pitch Template

rule.statement: Use the 7-line elevator pitch template to ensure every pitch covers: target customer, problem, category, key benefit, competitor contrast, and differentiator.
rule.rationale: Omitting any element produces a pitch that either confuses the audience about who this is for (no target), fails to establish urgency (no problem), or cannot be remembered (no differentiator).

section.reference: Full elevator pitch template with 7-line and 10-second versions

rule.statement: Match pitch structure to the audience role: executives need business impact first, technical audiences need architecture before benefits, finance needs cost savings with payback period.
rule.rationale: Each role has a primary decision filter. Leading with the wrong frame (e.g., technical architecture to an executive) triggers disengagement before the key point lands.

list.item: Technical: Problem (1m) → Architecture (2m) → Demo (5m) → Integration (2m) → Questions
Finance: Cost savings → TCO analysis → Payback period → Risk
Users: Time savings → Ease of use → Daily workflow change
Security: Compliance → Data protection → Audit trail

rule.statement: Use LAER (Listen, Acknowledge, Explore, Respond) for every objection — never skip Explore before Responding.
rule.rationale: Responding before exploring treats the stated objection as the real one. The real objection is usually different: "too expensive" often means "I don't see the ROI" not "we have no budget".

example.code: Objection: "Too expensive" L: Let them finish. A: "That's a valid concern." E: "Compared to what? What's the cost of not solving this?" R: "Let's calculate the TCO including the current manual cost..."
Objection: "We tried before" E: "What was different then? What would need to change for it to work now?"

rule.statement: Structure presentations as: Hook → Problem → Solution → Proof → Call to Action. End with a specific ask, never an open-ended close.
rule.rationale: Presentations without a clear ask produce "let me think about it" deferrals. The ask defines what decision the audience is making.

antipattern.description: Leading with the solution before establishing the problem.
antipattern.reason: Audiences who do not yet feel the pain perceive solutions as unnecessary complexity. Pain must be established first.

antipattern.description: Asking "Does that make sense?" after presenting.
antipattern.reason: Invites a yes/no response that shuts down dialogue. Use "What questions do you have?" instead.

antipattern.description: Using weak modal verbs: "I think we should...", "Maybe we could..."
antipattern.reason: Modality signals uncertainty, which undermines the recommendation. Use "I recommend..." and "Here's the plan..."
